coredata.dc:title: Electro-enhanced adsorption of lead ions from slightly-polluted water by capacitive deionization
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Ranran
Xu, Bin
Chen, Yun
Yin, Xiaoshuang
Liu, Ying
Yang, Wenzhong
coredata.dc:description: Capacitive deionization (CDI) technology is an effective method for the removal of Pb2+ from slightly-polluted water. In this study, reduced graphene oxide/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (rGO/NCDs) composites with different NCDs mass ratios were successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method and the preparation process of CDI electrode was optimized by electrospinning method to obtain monolithic mesh-like structure. The results showed that the doping of NCDs improved the structural and electrochemical properties of the composites. rGO/25%NCDs composite possessed well-developed three-dimensional porous structure, high specific surface area (347.65 m2/g) and excellent specific capacitance (284.0 F/g). The prepared CDI electrodes had a fish scale-like graded interfacial structure, and this unique structure made the electrodes exhibit superhydrophilic property. The results of CDI tests showed that the electrosorption capacity of rGO/25%NCDs electrode could reach 19.26 mg/g which was higher than that of the pure rGO electrode and many reported electrodes, when the initial concentration of Pb2+ was 5 mg/L and the voltage was −1.0 V. The adsorption process was consistent with the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. In addition, the study on the competitive adsorption in a co-existed system revealed that the electrosorption capacity was strongly dependent on the ionic valence rather than hydrated radius.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption
Graphene
Heavy metals
Capacitive deionization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of rGO and rGO/NCDs composites
Properties of NCDs and rGO/NCDs composites
Electrochemical properties of rGO and rGO/NCDs composites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the NCDs (insert shows the corresponding size histogram of the NCDs).
SEM images of rGO (a), rGO/15%NCDs (b), rGO/25%NCDs (c) and rGO/35%NCDs (d) composites.
(a) TEM image, (b) High-resolution TEM image of the rGO/25%NCDs composite. The inset in (b): High-resolution TEM image showing lattice spacing of NCDs.
XRD patterns of rGO, rGO/15%NCDs, rGO/25%NCDs and rGO/35%NCDs composites.
Nitrogen adsorption–desorption isotherms of rGO (a), rGO/15%NCDs (b), rGO/25%NCDs (c) and rGO/35%NCDs (d) composites. The inset in (a-d) were pore size distribution curves.
CV curves for rGO (a), rGO/15%NCDs (b), rGO/25%NCDs (c) and rGO/35%NCDs (d) composites at various scan rates.
(a) Specific capacitance at different scan rates, and (b) GCD curves at 1 A g−1 of the rGO, rGO/15%NCDs, rGO/25%NCDs and rGO/35%NCDs electrodes.
(a) SEM image of rGO/25%NCDs CDI electrode, and (b) the corresponding magnification of rGO/25%NCDs CDI electrode.

Electrosorption capacity of rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes with respect to different operation conditions: (a) Applied voltage. The flow rate was 20 mL/min and initial Pb2+ concentration was 5 mg/L. (b) Initial Pb2+ concentration. The applied voltage was −1.0 V and flow rate was 20 mL/min. (c) Flow rate. The applied voltage was −1.0 V and initial Pb2+ concentration was 5 mg/L. (d)The changes of Pb2+ concentration under different flow rates for rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes.
Regenerated electrosorption capacities of rGO electrode (a) and rGO/25%NCDs electrode (b).
Electrosorption isotherms of Pb2+ on the electrodes, (a) Langmuir isotherm, (b) Freundlich isotherm.
Electrosorption kinetics of Pb2+ on the electrodes, (a, b) pseudo-first-order model, (c, d) pseudo-second-order model.

(a) The normalized equivalent electrical adsorption capacities of different metal ions at rGO/25%NCDs electrode in a single electrolyte solution. (b) The concentration changes of the co-existed electrosorption system.
XPS spectra of (a) rGO/25%NCDs electrode before and after the adsorption of Pb2+, high-resolution scan of (b) Pb4f, (c) N1s before and after the adsorption of Pb2+.
BET and EIS results of rGO and rGO/NCDs composites.
Parameters determined by fitting of Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms for CDI performance of rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes.
Parameters determined by fitting of electrosorption kinetic models for CDI performance of rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes.
The charge size and hydrated radius of each heavy metal ion.

head.ce:title: Electro-enhanced adsorption of lead ions from slightly-polluted water by capacitive deionization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, No. 30 Puzhu Road (S), Nanjing 211816, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Nanjing Tech University, No. 30 Puzhu Road (S), Nanjing 211816, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanjing Institute of Environmental Sciences, Ministry of Ecology and Environment of the People's Republic of China, No. 8 Jiangwangmiao Street, Nanjing 210042, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ministry of Ecology and Environment of the People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanjing Institute of Environmental Sciences, Ministry of Ecology and Environment of the People's Republic of China, No. 8 Jiangwangmiao Street, Nanjing 210042, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Capacitive deionization (CDI) technology is an effective method for the removal of Pb2+ from slightly-polluted water. In this study, reduced graphene oxide/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (rGO/NCDs) composites with different NCDs mass ratios were successfully synthesized by hydrothermal method and the preparation process of CDI electrode was optimized by electrospinning method to obtain monolithic mesh-like structure. The results showed that the doping of NCDs improved the structural and electrochemical properties of the composites. rGO/25%NCDs composite possessed well-developed three-dimensional porous structure, high specific surface area (347.65 m2/g) and excellent specific capacitance (284.0 F/g). The prepared CDI electrodes had a fish scale-like graded interfacial structure, and this unique structure made the electrodes exhibit superhydrophilic property. The results of CDI tests showed that the electrosorption capacity of rGO/25%NCDs electrode could reach 19.26 mg/g which was higher than that of the pure rGO electrode and many reported electrodes, when the initial concentration of Pb2+ was 5 mg/L and the voltage was −1.0 V. The adsorption process was consistent with the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. In addition, the study on the competitive adsorption in a co-existed system revealed that the electrosorption capacity was strongly dependent on the ionic valence rather than hydrated radius.

ce:section.ce:para: Lead ion (Pb2+) is one of the most toxic metals that are widely used in various industrial activities, such as mineral smelting, battery manufacturing, metal plating and finishing, printing and tanning. The permissible level for lead in drinking water by Chinese Standards (GB 5749-2006) is fixed at 10 ppb. Therefore, it is of great importance to remove Pb2+ from slightly-polluted water. Currently, traditional methods for Pb2+ removal include chemical precipitation [1], ion exchange [2], membrane separation [3], electrolysis [4], biosorption [5] and so on. However, these methods are difficult to treat slightly-polluted water with low Pb2+ concentration, and have shortcomings of high cost, prone to secondary pollution, high technical requirements and severe conditions. In contrast, capacitive deionization (CDI) is a promising technology for removing Pb2+ from slightly-polluted water. This technology has advantages of high efficiency, green energy saving, no secondary pollution, simple operating conditions and regenerable electrodes [6–8].

CDI technology is an electrosorption technology based on the electrochemical double layer capacitance theory. The design and construction of electrode material is the key to CDI technology. At present, carbon-based materials are used as the conventional CDI electrode materials. Among them, honeycomb lattice graphene materials formed by conjugated carbon atoms are exploited as the feasible electrode materials for Pb2+ removal due to their excellent electrical conductivity, specific surface area and capacitive properties [9–13]. However, the strong van der Waals force between adjacent sheets leads to aggregation and restacking of graphene [14,15], which reduces the ion adsorption effect [16,17]. Carbon quantum dots, composed of sp2/sp3 carbon core and outer layer of oxygen/nitrogen functional groups or polymer aggregates, are new zero-dimensional carbon materials with size less than 10 nm. In recent years, carbon quantum dots have shown excellent energy storage and conversion performance in the field of batteries and supercapacitors [18,19]. Therefore, nanocomposites based on carbon quantum dots have great potential in capacitive deionization applications due to their excellent electrochemical performance. It is noteworthy that the introduction of heteroatoms can modify the electron state and electron migration properties of carbon quantum dots and produce more active sites. Hence, with a combination of zero-dimensional nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and two-dimensional graphene to construct three-dimensional spatial structure can increase the specific surface area and improve electrochemical properties. As far as we know, reduced graphene oxide/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (rGO/NCDs) composites have seldom been studied as capacitive deionization electrode materials.

The optimization of CDI electrode preparation process is another important approach to improve electrosorption efficiency of Pb2+. Optimizing the electrode preparation process and regulating the micro-interface structure of electrode materials can improve the capacitive deionization performance. Currently, conventional CDI electrodes are made by pressing a mixture of electrode material, binder and conductive agent [20,21]. However, this preparation method often fails to obtain electrodes with acceptable electrosorption capacity due to high resistance and hydrophobicity of the binder, which tends to cause the loss of active electrode materials and increase the internal resistance of the whole device [22–24]. The electrospinning method is simple and environmentally friendly, by which flexible electrodes with macroscopic monolithic mesh electrode can be effectively prepared. CDI electrodes prepared by electrospinning method can be directly used for electrosorption experiments, which makes up for the disadvantage of employing a binder and also promotes CDI technology one step further toward large-scale applications. In addition, the desalination performance of CDI can be improved by employing electrodes with high hydrophilicity [24–26]. Biomimetic studies have shown that many superhydrophilic structures exist in nature, such as the layered structure of oriented micropapillae on the surface of fish scales, the radial groove ridge structure on the leaves of nepenthes pitcher plant, and the porous sponge-like structure of sphagnum moss. The surface roughness and porosity are responsible for these superhydrophilic surfaces [27,28]. Meanwhile, fibrous membranes prepared by electrospinning methods often have high porosity, high specific surface area and easily adjustable structures. To the best of our knowledge, there is no similar report on the design of interfacial structures by electrospinning method for their application in CDI. Therefore, it is expected that the electrospinning method can be used to construct micro/nano-rough and porous interfacial structures to improve the wettability of electrodes and reduce the resistance for ion transport, which can help improve the electrosorption efficiency of Pb2+ removal by CDI electrodes.

Herein, the feasibility of CDI for removing Pb2+ from slightly-polluted water was demonstrated. Novel three-dimensional rGO/NCDs composites with large specific surface area and excellent electrochemical properties were developed, while a monolithic mesh electrode with a fish-scale interface structure was innovatively prepared. Such unique structure can greatly improve the wettability of the electrode, which was beneficial to improve the electrosorption efficiency of CDI. More specifically, the effects of different factors on the Pb2+ electrosorption efficiency were studied to determine the optimal adsorption conditions. The isotherm model and kinetic model were established to evaluate the Pb2+ electrosorption behavior according to the experimental results. Additional experiments were conducted to evaluate the effect of co-existing ions on the electrosorption performance of lead removal. The obtained essential parameters in this research can be extended to further full-scale CDI application.

The sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98%), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%), anhydrous ethanol, lead chloride (PbCl2), sodium chloride (NaCl), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), ferric chloride (FeCl3) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.5%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Polyamindoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid anhydrous was bought from Aladdin. Polyacrylonitrile (PAN, average MW150000) was purchased from J&K Scientific Co. Ltd., Beijing, China. Deionized water was used in this study to prepare all solutions, and all the purchased materials were used without any purification.

The NCDs were prepared by hydrothermal method. 1.0 g of citric acid and 1.0 mL of PAMAM dendrimer were dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water. After homogeneous mixing, the solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The supernatant was removed and the products were purified by centrifugation, filtration, dialysis and spin evaporation. Finally, the purified mixture was dried under vacuum in a freeze-drying oven for 24 h to obtain NCDs brown powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of rGO and rGO/NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: GO was prepared from natural graphite powder using a modified Hummers method [29]. The prepared GO suspension (2 mg/L) was mixed with a certain amount of NCDs powder under ultrasonic action. The mixture was then placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction was completed, the autoclave was cooled naturally to room temperature. The resulting rGO/NCDs hydrogel was taken out from the autoclave and freeze-dried at −40 °C to obtain rGO/NCDs composites. The rGO/NCDs were named as rGO/15%NCDs, rGO/25%NCDs and rGO/35%NCDs where the percentage indicates the amount of NCDs powder. Pure rGO was also prepared by the same process without the addition of NCDs powder.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss Ultra 55) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Jeol JEM 2100plus) were used to observe the surface morphology and structures of NCDs, rGO and rGO/NCDs materials. The phase composition of the material was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Thermo Scientific, Cu Kα radiation, λ = 1.54051 Å) under copper target testing within a scan range of 5–80°. The layer spacing of the sample can be calculated according to the Bragg equation (2dsinθ = nλ), where d is the crystal plane spacing, θ is the angle between the incident X-rays and the corresponding crystal plane, n is the number of diffraction levels, and λ is the wavelength of the X-rays. The specific surface area and pore structure of the materials were determined using a fully automated specific surface and porosity analyzer (BET, Mac2460). The water contact angle of the electrodes was measured using a DSA100-x instrument. The elemental valence states of the materials were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). A three-electrode system with 20 mg/L of PbCl2 as the electrolyte, saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode and platinum foil as the counter electrode was used for the electrochemical analysis. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was analyzed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E, Shanghai Chenhua) with a scanning potential range of −1.0 V–0 V. A constant current charge/discharge test (GCD) with a current density of 1 A/g was used. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were measured at the open circuit potential over the frequency range of 100 mHz ∼ 100 kHz with a 5 mV peak-to-peak voltage excitation.

The CDI electrode, namely rGO/NCDs composite nanofiber, was fabricated by electrospinning. The spinning solution was prepared by dissolving 1.0 g of PAN and 0.2 g of rGO/NCDs materials in 10 mL of DMF solution overnight at 60 °C under stirring condition. In a typical manufacturing process, the spinning solution was injected into a 10 mL syringe, and then the electrospinning voltage was adjusted to 18 kV. The distance between the receiving plate and the needle was 15 cm, and the injection pump speed was 1 mL/h. In the absence of NCDs doping, pure rGO composite nanofibers were prepared by the same procedure. Finally, the composite nanofibers were cut into 4 × 4 cm2 sheets and pressed on the ethanol-treated nickel foam as CDI electrodes for subsequent Pb2+ electrosorption experiments.

The CDI performances of the electrodes were evaluated with a batch-mode experimental system including a CDI unit cell, an external power supply, a peristaltic pump and a reservoir. The CDI unit cell consisted of support plates, rubber gaskets, CDI electrodes and insulating gauze, which was similar to the pervious reported cells [30,31]. Before the beginning of the electrosorption experiments, the CDI electrodes were cleaned with deionized water to remove impurities and then immersed in 5 mg/L of PbCl2 solution overnight to minimize the impact of physical adsorption. In each experiment, a given potential was applied to the CDI cell and the feed solution (80 mL) was continuously circulated through the CDI unit cell by a peristaltic pump until the Pb2+ concentration remained constant. The desorption process was realized via a reverse polarity method for about 30 min. The lead ions in the feed solution were continuously removed cycle by cycle. In this paper, the impacts of applied voltage, flow rate and initial concentration on Pb2+ removal were studied and each experiment was repeated with triplicate. When the solution passed through the CDI cell, the anions (Cl-) and cations (Pb2+) were adsorbed on the anode and cathode, respectively, to achieve the goal of electrosorption. The concentration of Pb2+ was determined by an atomic absorption spectrophotometer. The electrosorption capacity (Q e, mg/g) and removal efficiency (η, %) were calculated by the following equations:
(1)
Q e
=
(
C 0
-
C e
) V
m
(2)
η =
C 0
-
C e
C 0
× 100 %
where C 0 and C e represent the initial and equilibrated concentrations of Pb2+, respectively (mg/L), V is the volume of the solution (L), m is the mass of the active material in the electrode (0.09 ± 0.005 g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of NCDs and rGO/NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and particle size of the NCDs were firstly characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 , the prepared NCDs appeared as spherical particles with uniform dispersion and no obvious aggregation was seen. The corresponding particle size distribution map (Fig. 1, inset) showed that the NCDs were mainly distributed in the range of 2.0–4.0 nm, and their average particle size was calculated to be about 3.0 nm.

Fig. 2 depicted the SEM images of rGO, rGO/15%NCDs, rGO/25%NCDs and rGO/35%NCDs nanocomposites, respectively. Fig. 2a showed that pure rGO consisted of a two-dimensional lamellar structure with a large number of folds on the smooth surface. After doping with NCDs nanoparticles, it can be seen from Fig. 2b-d that the rGO/NCDs composites showed three-dimensional porous structures, and the diameters of pores first increased and then decreased with the increase of NCDs doping. The reason can be that when a small amount of NCDs was doped, the NCDs particles effectively prevent the aggregation of graphene nanosheets and presented a three-dimensional porous structure. Before the NCDs doping amount reached 25%, the porous structure was further improved. However, when the NCDs doping amount continued to increase, excessive NCDs particles may agglomerate and cover the pores on the surface of the composite or fill inside the pore structure, causing the reduction of pore structure [32]. Therefore, the rGO/25%NCDs composite had the best three-dimensional porous structure, facilitating the increase of specific surface area and ion transport adsorption rate, which in turn increased the adsorption capacity of Pb2+ [33].

As shown in Fig. 3 a, it was obvious that a batch of NCDs was decorated on the surface of graphene sheets. Elemental dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) elemental mapping (Fig. S1) presented a uniform distribution of N element, indicating the successful introduction of N atoms into the carbon skeleton. Fig. 3b depicted the high-resolution TEM image of rGO/25%NCDs, where the striation pattern in the lattice of NCDs can be clearly observed, and the calculated lattice spacing of 0.21 nm corresponded to the (100) plane of graphite.

The crystal structure and chemical composition of the samples were analyzed by XRD. As shown in Fig. 4 , the (001) diffraction peak observed at 9.8° confirmed the formation of GO. According to the Bragg equation (2dsinθ = nλ), the calculated layer spacing was 0.9 nm, which was much larger than that of pristine graphite (0.34 nm), indicating the presence of a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface [34]. After the hydrothermal process, the typical (001) peak almost disappeared and a broad diffraction peak (002) near 21.9° appeared in rGO and rGO/NCDs composites, indicating the effective reduction of the material. In addition, the (002) diffraction peak of the rGO/NCDs composites was gradually shifted to higher angles with increasing NCDs loading, which was due to the π-π interaction with graphitized NCDs [35]. The width of the peaks also increased with increasing loading of NCDs, which indicated the increase of defects or disordered structures in graphene nanosheets. These defects or disordered structures can provide more active sites for Pb2+ electrosorption [36].

The specific surface area and pore structure of rGO and rGO/NCDs composites were investigated by N2 adsorption/desorption experiments. As can be seen from Fig. 5 , the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms of all samples had similar contours and can be classified as type IV isotherms [37]. When P/P 0 > 0.4, the N2 adsorption and desorption curves of both rGO and rGO/NCDs composites appeared hysteretic loops, indicating that both rGO and rGO/NCDs composites had mesoporous structures. The presence of a large number of mesoporous structures can effectively promote the rapid penetration of electrolyte ions and improve the capacitance behavior, so as to enhance the adsorption capacity of the electrode [38]. The specific surface area, pore volume and average pore diameter of all samples were listed in Table 1 . Compared with pure rGO, the specific surface area of NCDs modified graphene was increased, because the addition of NCDs reduced the aggregation effect between graphene sheets. In addition, the average pore size and pore volume of rGO/NCDs composites were also improved compared with that of pure rGO. Among all the samples, the specific surface area, average pore size and pore volume of rGO/25%NCDs material were the largest, indicating that the addition of NCDs with appropriate amount can effectively improve the specific surface area and pore structure of the composite. Thus, rGO/25%NCDs material provided more active sites to increase the adsorption capacity of Pb2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of rGO and rGO/NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para:
The electrochemical properties of rGO and rGO/NCDs were evaluated by CV, GCD and EIS tests. Fig. 6 showed the CV curves of rGO and rGO/NCDs at different scan rates. As can be seen from the figure, the CV curves of all samples were approximately rectangular with no obvious redox peaks at scan rates of 10–100 mV/s, suggesting a typical electrical double layer behavior [31]. As the scan rate increased, the closed area of the CV curves increased and the shape deviated slightly to some extent, showing excellent rate capability [39]. The specific capacitance (C s) is an important index to evaluate the electrochemical properties of electrode materials, which can be calculated by the formula:
(3)
C s
=
∫
IdV
2 v m Δ V
where C s is the specific capacitance (F/g), I is the response current (A), V is the potential (V), v is the potential scan rate (V/s).

Fig. 7 a depicted the specific capacitance of the prepared composites at different scan rates. It was apparent that the specific capacitance of rGO and rGO/NCDs materials decreased with increasing scan rate. The specific capacitance of rGO/NCDs composites was larger than that of graphene at different scan rates. This phenomenon suggested that the doping of NCDs can improve the specific capacitance of rGO/NCDs composites, which may be due to the optimization of the pore structure and improvement of the specific surface area, while the introduction of N element can improve the electron transport properties [40]. The specific capacitance of rGO, rGO/15%NCDs, rGO/25%NCDs and rGO/35%NCDs were 104.5, 153.3, 284.0, and 186.4 F/g, respectively, when the scan rate was 10 mV/s. It was worth noting that the specific capacitance of rGO/35%NCDs was lower than that of rGO/25%NCDs, which was due to the occurrence of agglomeration phenomenon, causing a decrease in the specific surface area of rGO/35%NCDs and affecting the contact area of the rGO/35%NCDs material with the electrolyte solution [41]. Among the specific capacitance of all composites, it was obvious that rGO/25%NCDs had the largest specific capacitance, indicating that the best electrosorption performance of the composites can be achieved when the NCDs doping ratio was 25%. Fig. 7b showed the GCD plots of rGO and rGO/NCDs composites at current density of 1 A/g. From Fig. 7b, it can be seen that the charging and discharging curves approximated isosceles triangles, and maintained a high degree of symmetry, further indicating that the charging and discharging of the materials were reversible [42]. With the longest discharge time, rGO/25%NCDs composite performed the best capacitive performance [43,44], which was consistent with the results of the CV characterization analysis described above.

Fig. S2 showed the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) images of the rGO and rGO/NCDs composites. In the electrochemical impedance plots, the part of the high-frequency region forming an approximate semicircle corresponded to the charge transfer resistance Rct of the material [45]. The Rct values of rGO and rGO/NCDs composites were listed in Table 1. It can be seen from Table 1 that Rct values of rGO/NCDs composites were lower than that of rGO, indicating the doping of NCDs nanoparticles can effectively prevent the agglomeration between the graphene lamellae, thus reducing the internal resistance and improving the ion transfer rate. In addition, the straight line in the low frequency region reflected the capacitive properties of the electrode [46]. The larger the slope of the straight line, the faster ion diffusion behavior of the material. It can be seen that rGO/25%NCDs composite had the lowest Rct value and the steepest linear gradient, indicating that it had the best electrochemical performance.
In summary, we selected rGO/25%NCDs composites with excellent structural and electrochemical properties for downstream CDI experiments.

The microstructure of CDI electrode membranes (rGO/NCDs) prepared by electrospinning method was characterized by SEM. It can be seen that the CDI electrode fiber membranes showed random orientation with porous structure (Fig. 8 a). rGO sheets existed on the electrode surface in three forms: wrapped, embedded and free between adjacent fibers. It was worth noting that the existence of these three forms can make rGO, NCDs and PAN fibers form a “point-line-surface” network structure. Such a network structure was conducive to electron transport and ion migration, which will greatly improve the conductivity of the electrode, and be beneficial to electrosorption. As can be seen from the enlarged view of Fig. 8b, the electrode surface showed a graded roughness structure at the micro/nanometer level accompanied by random papilla-like protrusions, similar to a fish scale surface. The reason for this structure was ascribe to that the presence of functional groups on GO substrate changed the electronic structure of carbon atoms from sp2 hybridization to sp3 hybridization. Consequently, carbon atoms were deviated from their original planes, causing such atomic-level ripples [47].

As we all know, a well-wetted electrode material can make full use of the pore structure and improve the performance of CDI [24–26]. In order to evaluate the wetting performance on the CDI electrode membrane, contact angle measurements were carried out, as shown in Fig. S3. The contact angle decreased with increasing wetting time when water droplets were placed on the electrode membrane. The water contact angle of the rGO electrode membrane decreased from 134° initially to 84° within 15 s. By comparison, the water contact angle of the rGO/NCDs electrode membrane decreased more rapidly, and even the water contact angle of rGO/35%NCDs reached a superhydrophilicity of 0° within 6 s.

The CDI electrode membrane exhibited excellent wetting performance. On the one hand, this is due to the presence of electronegative N element in the electrode material itself [48]. On the other hand, more importantly, a biomimetic fish scale-like graded structure was constructed on the electrode surface [49]. This unique structure allowed slightly-polluted water to quickly penetrate into the various pores of the electrode when it flowed into the interior of the electrode, thus enabling efficient adsorption of Pb2+ from slightly-polluted water.
To estimate the impacts of applied voltage, flow rate and initial ion concentration on Pb2+ electrosorption behavior of the fabricated electrodes, batch mode experiments were conducted.

The applied voltage is an important factor affecting the electrosorption performance of a CDI reactor. In this study, the electrosorption experiments for lead removal were conducted by applying −0.6 V, −0.8 V and −1.0 V to the rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the Q e of Pb2+ increased with the increase of applied voltage. When the applied voltage was −1.0 V, the maximum Q e of Pb2+ removal by rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes reached 15.05 mg/g and 19.26 mg/g, respectively. This was because that the higher applied voltage led to stronger Coulomb interaction, and the formation of thicker electrical double layer at the electrode-solution interface, so more lead ions were adsorbed at the electrosorption equilibrium [50]. Meanwhile, by comparing the Q e values of rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes at different voltages, it was found that the Q e values increased significantly after NCDs doped. This indicated that the rGO/25%NCDs electrode, possessing high specific surface area and enhanced electrochemical properties, can exhibit great electrosorption performance.

The electrosorption variation patterns of the CDI electrode were investigated at different initial Pb2+ concentrations (1, 5, 10, 20 mg/L) under the voltage of −1.0 V. As shown in Fig. 9b, the Q e value gradually increased when the initial Pb2+ concentration increased from 1 mg/L to 20 mg/L. And the maximum Q e values of rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes were 26.52 and 31.74 mg/g respectively when the Pb2+ concentration was 20 mg/L. This was because higher initial concentration caused more contact opportunities between the electrode material and Pb2+. In addition, the high ionic strength can promote the diffusion and transfer of lead ions, so the initial concentration of Pb2+ was proportional to the Q e value [49]. In contrast, the line graph in Fig. 9b showed that the removal efficiency obtained according to Eq. (2) was inversely related to the initial Pb2+ concentration. This was ascribed to that the increased Pb2+ initial concentration was much larger than the maximum saturation value that can be adsorbed by the electrode, so a decrease in adsorption efficiency was shown [30,51].

In order to study the influence of the flow rate on the CDI process, three different flow rates (20, 30, 40 mL/min) were set for the CDI tests, and the results were shown in Fig. 9c. The adsorption capacities of Pb2+ by rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes decreased slowly with the increase of the flow rate, and the maximum adsorption capacities of Pb2+ by the electrodes were observed at the flow rate of 20 mL/min. The high inlet flow rate will scour the electrical double layer to make the adsorbed Pb2+ desorb into the solution. In addition, it can be seen from Fig. 9d that higher flow rate caused less time for the electrode to reach the adsorption equilibrium, which was due to the shortened residence time of the solution inside the electrode, and thus the ions were flushed out before forming a stable electrical double layer [52]. The electrosorption capacity of Pb2+ by rGO/25%NCDs electrode was higher than that of pure rGO electrode at different flow rate, indicating that the NCDs-modified graphene electrode had stronger stability and better pore structure.

In practical applications, the regeneration ability of CDI electrodes was crucial. In order to evaluate the regeneration stability of rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes, cyclic electrosorption-desorption experiments were performed in 5 mg/L of Pb2+ solution. As shown in Fig. 10 , it can be found that at the early stage of cyclic adsorption and desorption, the electrosorption capacity of Pb2+ increased slightly, which may be due to the activation of the active components in rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes during the process of electrosorption and desorption. The electrosorption capacity decreased from 15.05 to 13.65 mg/g for rGO electrode and from 19.26 to 18.03 mg/g for rGO/25%NCDs electrode after 10 cycles, which indicated that rGO/25%NCDs electrode had better stability for the removal of Pb2+ from slightly-polluted water. Moreover, many electrodes for Pb2+ removal have been reported, such as polypyrrole and air-plasma activated carbon nanotube composite, activated carbon cloth coated with zinc oxide nanoparticles, polyaniline/attapulgite composites, carbon nanotube treated by air-plasma electrodes, and their electrosorption capacities were 4.28, 15.67, 15.42, 2.4 mg/g, respectively [44,53–55]. In comparison, the electrosorption capacity of rGO/25%NCDs electrode was 19.26 mg/g when the voltage was −1.0 V and the initial concentration of Pb2+ was 5 mg/L in this work. This clearly demonstrated the outstanding electrosorption performance of rGO/25%NCDs electrode.

In order to study the isothermal adsorption behavior of Pb2+ removal by CDI electrode, Freundlich model and Langmuir model were used for fitting, which can be expressed by Eqs. (4) and (5) as follows [56].
(4)
Freundlich
ln
q e
= ln
K F
+
1 n
ln
C e
(5)
Langmuir
C e
q e
=
1
q m
K L
+
C e
q m
where C e is the equilibrium concentration (mg/L), q e is the amount of Pb2+ adsorbed at equilibrium (mg/g), q m is the maximum adsorption capacity (mg/g), K L is the Langmuir constant (L/mg), K F is the Freundlich constant (L/mg), 1/n (ranging between 0 and 1) is an indication of the adsorption tendency of the adsorbate.

The parameters and regression coefficients (R 2) of rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes for Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms were shown in Table 2 . As we all know, the Langmuir adsorption equation mainly described the monolayer adsorption on the surface of the adsorbent material, assuming a limited number of adsorption sites and uniform surface adsorption energy on the electrode surface, with no interaction between adsorbates [57]. Meanwhile, the Freundlich adsorption isotherm usually described the non-uniformity of the adsorption surface and the exponential distribution of adsorption sites and their energies. The adsorption can be extended to multilayer coverage [56]. According to the regression coefficients (R 2) of each isotherm fitted in Fig. 11 and Table 2, it was obvious that the electrosorption behavior of rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes for Pb2+ was more consistent with Langmuir adsorption isotherm, demonstrating the unimolecular layer adsorption and uniform surface adsorption energy. Furthermore, the theoretical maximum adsorption capacities (q m) of rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes were 29.48 and 32.06 mg/g, respectively, which were very close to the experimental values (26.52 mg/g, 31.74 mg/g at applied voltage −1.0 V). In addition, the 1/n values obtained from the Freundlich model fitting were 0.441 and 0.372, respectively, which were less than 1, indicating that the adsorption behaviors of both rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes for Pb2+ were relatively easy to achieve [58].

In addition to the adsorption isotherms, the kinetic model was also used to fit the adsorption behavior of Pb2+ on CDI electrode. The pseudo-first-order model and pseudo-second-order model were two common kinetic models and the equations were as follows [59,60]:
(6)
Pseudo - f i r s t - o r d e r
m o d e l :
q t
=
q e
-
q e
e
-
K 1
t
(7)
Pseudo - s e c o n d - o r d e r
m o d e l :
t
q t
=
1
K 2
q
e
2
+
t
q e
where q e and q t are Pb2+ adsorption capacity (mg/g) at equilibrium and at t (min) respectively; K 1 and K 2 are pseudo-first-order (min−1) and pseudo-second-order rate constants (g (mg min)-1) respectively.

Fig. 12 showed the fitting results of the kinetic model to the experimental data at different voltage. The fitted parameters and correlation coefficients were listed in Table 3 . It was obvious that both pseudo-first-order and pseudo-second-order models can be used to approximate the experimental data, but the pseudo-first-order model had a relatively high correlation. This result suggested that the physical adsorption of Pb2+ was dominated for rGO and rGO/25%NCDs electrodes, and the adsorption capacity was proportional to the voltage. When more negative voltage was applied to the electrode, the more charges gathered on the electrode surface and the electrostatic force on Pb2+ in solution was enhanced. In addition, the equilibrium electrosorption capacity of rGO/25%NCDs electrode was larger than that of rGO electrode at the same voltage, indicating that the modification of rGO by NCDs nanoparticles could effectively improve the CDI performance of the electrode.

Considering the coexistence of various metal ions in the actual polluted water, it was necessary to study the effect of competitive adsorption on Pb2+ removal performance. In this manuscript, four metal ions, Na+, Cd2+, Pb2+ and Fe3+, were selected for the electrosorption tests. Their charge sizes and hydrated radii were summarized in Table 4 . Fig. 13 a showed the normalized electrosorption capacity of different metal ion on rGO/25%NCDs electrode in a single electrolyte solution with the same initial concentration. It can be seen that the order of normalized adsorption capacity of each ion was: Fe3+>Pb2+>Cd2+>Na+. For Pb2+ and Cd2+ with the same valence state, the one with smaller hydrated radius was attracted. This was due to that the smaller hydrated radius was benefit for ion to enter into the micropores of the electrode material. For Fe3+, Pb2+ and Na+ with different valence state, the highly charged multivalent ions was attracted [58].

Fig. 13b showed the change of metal ion concentration in the co-existed electrosorption system when the initial concentration of each metal ion was 5.0 mg/L. The concentrations of Na+, Pb2+ and Fe3+ all decreased rapidly at the initial stage of electrosorption. Then, the concentrations of Pb2+ and Fe3+ continued to decrease, while the concentration of Na+ increased, indicating that some Na+ was replaced by Pb2+ and Fe3+. Therefore, for ions with different valence state, the electrostatic attraction was mainly determined by the valence state, not the hydrated radius [61].

In conclusion, for a single electrolyte solution, the ions with higher valence state and smaller hydrated radius had higher priority during electrical adsorption. For the mixed system with competitive adsorption among various metal ions, the effect of valence state on electrosorption selectivity was higher than that of hydrated radius.

To further understand the possible adsorption mechanism of Pb2+ on rGO/NCDs electrode, XPS measurements before and after electrosorption were carried out. Fig. 14 a showed the XPS broad sweep spectra of the rGO/NCDs electrode before and after the electrosorption of Pb2+, and the detection of lead element in the XPS pattern confirmed the adsorption of Pb2+. After a careful observation, it can be seen that the conjugate peaks existed in the narrow region scan of Pb4f (Fig. 14b). The corresponding peaks of Pb4f7/2 and Pb4f5/2 were at 138.74 and 143.56 eV, respectively, which indicated that there was a strong affinity between the adsorbed Pb2+ and the adsorbent, and the Pb was bivalent [62,63]. To further explore the adsorption mechanism, N1s high-resolution spectra was used to study the rGO/NCDs electrode before and after the adsorption of Pb2+. As shown in Fig. 14c, the binding energy (E) of N1s changed from 399.38 eV to 399.48 eV and the half-peak width (W) of N1s changed from 1.29 eV to 1.39 eV after the adsorption of Pb2+. Additionally, the intensity of N1s also showed a slight decrease. All these results indicated that N element was involved in the adsorption of Pb2+ [64,65]. The complex was formed by sharing lone-pair electrons in N atom with vacant orbital in Pb2+. In addition, N element can enhance wettability and thus improve utilization efficiency of the pores in electrode material. The peak of O1s in the electrode material was found to increase from 9.26% to 12.13% after the electrosorption experiment (Table S1), indicating that the reaction was accompanied by a slight oxidation reaction.

The present work demonstrated that CDI was feasible and effective for removing Pb2+ from slightly-polluted water. Novel rGO/NCDs electrode materials were synthesized by hydrothermal method, while monolithic mesh-like CDI electrode fiber membranes with a fish scale-like bionic hierarchical structure were prepared by electrospinning method. The Pb2+ removal by CDI depended on the applied voltage, initial concentration and flow rate. In addition, the electrosorption capacities for rGO/NCDs electrodes were superior to pure rGO electrode, and rGO/25%NCDs electrode performed the best capacity (19.26 mg/g). The Langmuir adsorption isotherm described the unimolecular layer adsorption behavior of Pb2+. Kinetic studies confirmed that the adsorption of Pb2+ on the CDI electrode was a physical adsorption process. Furthermore, the effect of co-existing cations was also investigated and selectivity of CDI was determined based on the hydrated radius and valence. XPS spectra demonstrated that N element was involved in the adsorption of lead ions and the electrosorption process was accompanied by a slight oxidation reaction. In summary, rGO/25%NCDs composites have a great potential for the treatment of Pb2+-containing slightly-polluted water by electrosorption, and the method of optimizing the electrode preparation process by electrospinning can further promote the wide application of CDI technology.

Ranran Wang: Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Bin Xu: Formal analysis, Data curation, Resources. Yun Chen: Formal analysis, Resources. Xiaoshuang Yin: Formal analysis, Data curation. Ying Liu: Validation. Wenzhong Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision.
